FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, baseline (Y0; ages 9-10) and year two follow-up (Y2; ages 11-12); US-based longitudinal study

FT_IC2.evidence: Addictive screen use composite measure including addictive videogaming, additive social media use, and addictive phone use; qualifies under B1 (separate coefficients for multiple platform/app-category measures)

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects regression with site and familyID as random intercepts; AFC (Anchored Functional Connectomic maturation score) predicting ASU composite, addictive videogaming, addictive social media use, and addictive phone use separately; regression coefficients reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Beta coefficients reported: AFCvs.max-ASU (β_M1 = -.056, CI [-.083, -.027], p < .001), AFCvs.addictive videogaming (β_M1 = -.060, CI [-.087, -.031], p < .001), AFCvs.addictive phone use (β_M1 = -.039, CI [-.069, -.008], p = .035); NAc activation (β_WE = -.023, CI [-.045, -.000], p = .029)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD analysis; longitudinal neuroimaging (fMRI) predicting behavioral outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal pre-proof (not a duplicate); original published manuscript

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply because ASU is the DEPENDENT variable, but the analysis includes non-demographic predictors (neural features: cortical connectomic maturation, NAc anticipatory reward activation, cognitive task performance, total screen time, attention problems, general factor of psychopathology). Per B17/FT-EC7, inclusion of ADHD (attention), general psychopathology, and neural features disqualifies FT-EC7.
notes: Strong longitudinal neuroimaging study with SM as DV alongside other ASU measures; predicted from neural risk factors. Satisfies all IC criteria but EC7 does not apply due to non-demographic predictors.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study, U.S.-based, ages 12-13 (year 2 and year 3 follow-up)

FT_IC2.evidence: Problematic gaming measured at age 12; qualifies under B6 (gaming + separate coefficients for environmental predictors); includes measurement of problematic gaming as SM/gaming measure with separate statistical modeling

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed models examining associations between problematic gaming at year 2 and PLEs at year 3; interaction tests between problematic gaming and environmental variables (peer, school, family); moderation analysis conducted

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression estimates with 95% CIs provided in Figure 2 and main text: School environment main effect (estimate -0.15, 95% CI [-0.21, -0.10]); Family environment main effect (estimate -2.37, 95% CI [-2.69, -2.05]); Interaction effects for problematic gaming x school environment and problematic gaming x family environment reported

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD analysis; prospective cohort study with problematic gaming as predictor of PLEs outcome

FT_IC6.evidence: Published full-length report; no indication of duplicate (preprint/abstract)

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply because problematic gaming is the INDEPENDENT/PREDICTOR variable (not DV), with PLEs as outcome. EC7 requires SM/gaming as DV only. Per B18, when paper has SM-as-IV→outcome analysis, FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Longitudinal study using affordance theory framework. Problematic gaming is predictor; school/family environment and PLEs are outcomes. Clear IV-DV structure disqualifies EC7.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study, U.S.-based; 2,294 participants, baseline (ages 9-10) to four-year follow-up (ages 13-14)

FT_IC2.evidence: Problematic use of social media (PUSM) measured via Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ, 6-item); qualifies as qualifying SM variable per definition (SMAQ/SNS-A/Bergen SM scales explicitly listed)

FT_IC3.evidence: Connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM) with 10-fold cross-validation; linear regression of SMAQ scores on functional connectivity edge weights; Pearson correlation between observed and predicted scores; permutation testing (1000 permutations)

FT_IC4.evidence: Pearson r correlations reported: combined positive and negative networks (r = 0.066, p = 0.003 unadjusted; r = 0.055, p = 0.016 controlling for covariates); permutation p = 0.02; connectivity edge counts: 2507 edges total (1212 positive, 1295 negative); full network anatomical descriptions and edge weights provided

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD analysis; machine-learning neuroimaging study predicting PUSM severity from baseline resting-state fMRI connectivity

FT_IC6.evidence: Published NeuroImage article; not a duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply because while PUSM is the DV, the predictors include neural features (resting-state functional connectivity from CPM), which is not in the closed demographic-only set per FT-EC7 (b). Per B17, neural features disqualify EC7.
notes: Machine-learning CPM study using whole-brain functional connectivity to predict PUSM. Includes traditional regression coefficients (counts as penalized regression equivalent per B8). Neural predictors disqualify EC7.
